participants.reasoning: The extracted criteria add several restrictions not stated officially, including a requirement to accept a possible 12-week waiting period and broader exclusion wording about repeated ambulatory monitoring, so the descriptions are not fully aligned.
participants.extracted: Adults aged 20 to 75 years with unmedicated stage 1 hypertension, identified by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (daytime mean systolic blood pressure at least 135 mm Hg or diastolic blood pressure at least 85 mm Hg, or 24-hour mean blood pressure at least 130/80 mm Hg). Participants had to be willing and able to take part in the 8-week mindfulness-based stress reduction programme, attend study visits and follow safety blood pressure checks, and accept a possible 12-week waiting period. Key exclusions included use of antihypertensive medication within the prior 6 months, very high blood pressure, diabetes, secondary hypertension, renal disease, prior myocardial infarction or stroke/transient ischemic attack, revascularisation, active malignancy, seizure within 6 months, congestive heart failure, severe liver disease, pregnancy or lactation, recent trial participation, planned elective surgery during the study period, or inability/unwillingness to complete repeated ambulatory blood pressure monitoring over 6 to 10 months.
participants.official:
Participants were recruited from referring physicians, advertisements in local newspapers, and posters at local hospitals. Recruitment dates not reported.
Inclusion criteria:
Eligible participants were aged 20 to 75 years with mean awake ambulatory systolic or diastolic BP ≥ 135/85 mmHg or mean 24‐hour ambulatory BP ≥ 130/80 mmHg. BP was required to be < 160/100 mmHg on both office and ambulatory measurements. Participants were naive to antihypertensive medication for at least 6 months before th

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention matches the official description in duration, group size, session structure, homework practice, and core MBSR elements, with only minor paraphrasing and extra examples.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was an 8-week mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) programme, delivered as a standardised group therapy for 25–30 participants. It consisted of 10 sessions in total: an introductory session, eight weekly 2.5-hour sessions, and one 6-hour intensive silent retreat held between weeks 6 and 7. The programme included formal meditation, informal mindfulness practice in daily activities, psychoeducational activities, self-monitoring/reflection exercises, and group discussion. Participants were instructed to practice for 45 minutes daily at home using guided CDs, and to complete weekly homework logs documenting formal and informal practice. Mindfulness practices taught included the body scan, sitting meditation, mindful yoga, mindful walking, mindful eating, and loving-kindness meditation.
interventions.official:
Intervention(duration 9 weeks, setting ‐ all MBSR therapy is conducted at the University Health Network’s Toronto General Hospital, Ontario):
MBSR was delivered by 2 trained therapists to groups of 25 to 30 individuals. It consisted of 10 sessions over 9 weeks (introduction, 8 weekly 2.5‐hour sessions, and a 6‐hour session/silent retreat). outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include many additional secondary measures beyond the official outcomes, and the primary/secondary timing and listed blood pressure endpoints do not match exactly.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: mean awake ambulatory blood pressure (systolic and diastolic) and 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure (systolic and diastolic), measured by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring at the 12-week post-baseline primary assessment period (after completion of the 8-week MBSR program for the early intervention group, and after the 12-week wait-list period for the delayed control group). Secondary outcomes: night-time ambulatory blood pressure (systolic and diastolic) assessed by ambulatory blood pressure monitoring at study visits; proportion of participants achieving blood pressure targets (24-hour ABP <130/80 mm Hg and daytime ABP <135/85 mm Hg) at study visits; initiation of antihypertensive medical therapy during the study, recorded throughout follow-up; adverse events, recorded throughout the study period; body mass index and waist circumference, measured at study visits; office blood pressure (seated systolic and diastolic blood pressure measured with the BpTRU automated device), assessed at study visits; fasting serum measures including plasma glucose, HbA1c, total cholesterol, HDL-cholesterol, LDL-cholesterol, triglycerides, creatinine, electrolytes, and liver enzymes, collected at study visits; urine creatinine and microalbuminuria, collected at study visits; electrocardiogram findings to detect abnormal heart rhythms and left ventricular hypertrophy, assessed at study visits; and questionnaire-based measures including state-trait anger expression, anxiety and depression, perceived stress, psychosocial stress, exercise, job strain, mindfulness, clinical outcome/routine evaluation, and Toronto Mindfulness Scale, administered at baseline and at repeated study visits (pre-MBSR, post-MBSR, and study close-out; with the Toronto Mindfulness Scale administered after meditation during the second and seventh MBSR classes).
